county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Randolph County formed 1835 from Lawrence County. County seat is Pocahontas, on the Black River. Located in northeastern Arkansas bordering Missouri.
county_metadata.research_tips: Randolph County records at the County Clerk's office in Pocahontas are complete from 1835. Pocahontas, on the Black River, was a steamboat town and later a railroad center. The Randolph County Historical Society maintains research files. The county borders Missouri, and cross-border migration was common -- check Missouri county records (Ripley, Butler, Carter) for related families. The Eddie Mae Herron Center in Pocahontas has local history collections. The county had both Union and Confederate sympathies during the Civil War. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Cemetery transcriptions cover many rural family burial grounds in this Ozark border county.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Randolph County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Pocahontas holds deed books from 1835

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Saline County formed 1835 from Pulaski County. County seat is Benton. Named for the saline (salt) springs in the area. Located just southwest of Little Rock, now part of the Little Rock metropolitan area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Saline County records at the County Clerk's office in Benton are complete from 1835. Benton, just southwest of Little Rock, has grown significantly as a Little Rock suburb. The county was named for the saline (salt) springs in the area, which attracted early settlers. The Saline County History and Heritage Society maintains research files. The Saline County Library has local history and genealogy collections. The county's proximity to Little Rock and the state archives makes research convenient. Salt works records from the territorial period are at the Arkansas History Commission. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Saline County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Benton holds deed books from 1835

county_metadata.record_losses: 1862 Civil War destruction; courthouse burned during guerrilla raids
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Scott County formed 1833 from Crawford and Pope counties. County seat is Waldron. Located in the Ouachita Mountains near the Oklahoma border. The 1862 Civil War courthouse burning destroyed records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Scott County's courthouse was burned during Civil War guerrilla raids in 1862, destroying records. The County Clerk's office in Waldron holds post-war records. This remote Ouachita Mountains county was part of the frontier zone between white settlement and Choctaw territory. Waldron is near the Oklahoma border. The Scott County Historical Society maintains reconstructed records. For pre-war families, check parent county records (Crawford, Pope) and GLO land patents. The county's isolation means church records from small congregations are especially important. FamilySearch has microfilmed post-war county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Scott County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Waldron holds deed books from 1843

source_profiles.survival_notes: Early deed books damaged or destroyed; 1862 Civil War destruction; courthouse burned during guerrilla raids. Post-loss records at County Clerk's office are generally complete

county_metadata.record_losses: 1865 Civil War guerrilla raids scattered records; some were later recovered
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Searcy County formed 1838 from Marion County. County seat is Marshall. Located in the most remote part of the Ozarks. The 1865 Civil War guerrilla raids destroyed or scattered records. Strongly Unionist during the Civil War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Searcy County records were destroyed or scattered during Civil War guerrilla raids in 1865. The County Clerk's office in Marshall holds post-war records. This remote Ozark county, including parts of the Buffalo River valley, was strongly Unionist. The county's extreme isolation and rugged terrain made it one of the poorest in Arkansas. The Searcy County Historical Society maintains reconstructed records. The Gilbert General Store (a historic site) documents mountain commerce. For pre-war records, check parent county (Marion) and the Arkansas History Commission. Family and church cemeteries are critical sources. FamilySearch has post-war county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Searcy County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Marshall holds deed books from 1838

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Sebastian County formed 1851 from Crawford and Scott counties. County seat is Fort Smith (also Greenwood for the southern district). Fort Smith was a major military post and seat of the US District Court for the Western District of Arkansas.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sebastian County records at the County Clerk's office in Fort Smith are well-preserved from 1851. Fort Smith was a major military post (established 1817) and the seat of the US District Court for the Western District of Arkansas -- 'Hanging Judge' Isaac Parker's court (1875-1896) had jurisdiction over Indian Territory (Oklahoma). Fort Smith National Historic Site archives hold federal court records. The Fort Smith Historical Society and the Fort Smith Museum of History have extensive collections. Sebastian County borders Oklahoma, and many families have ties to Indian Territory. Greenwood serves as a second district seat. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Sebastian County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Fort Smith holds deed books from 1851

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Sevier County formed 1828 from Hempstead County. County seat is De Queen. Named for Ambrose Hundley Sevier, one of Arkansas's first US senators. Located in southwest Arkansas bordering Oklahoma.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sevier County records at the County Clerk's office in De Queen are complete from 1828. This southwest Arkansas county borders Oklahoma and was part of the Choctaw removal zone. De Queen was a railroad town established by the Kansas City Southern Railway in the 1890s. The county was named for Ambrose Hundley Sevier, one of Arkansas's first US senators. The Sevier County Historical Society maintains research files. Choctaw removal records at NARA and the Oklahoma Historical Society are relevant. The Southwest Arkansas Regional Archives (SARA) holds regional materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Sevier County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in De Queen holds deed books from 1828

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Sharp County formed 1868 from Lawrence County. County seat is Ash Flat (moved from Hardy in 1967). Located in the Ozarks. Named for Ephraim Sharp, a state legislator.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sharp County records at the County Clerk's office in Ash Flat are complete from 1868. This Ozark county was carved from Lawrence County. Hardy, the original county seat, is a historic town on the Spring River. The Sharp County Heritage Museum in Hardy has local materials. The county's Ozark location means small Baptist and Church of Christ congregations hold the primary church records. Cemetery transcriptions are important for this rural county. The county was a strongly Unionist area during the Civil War. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check Lawrence County records for pre-1868 families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Sharp County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Ash Flat holds deed books from 1868

county_metadata.record_losses: 1858 fire and 1863 Civil War losses damaged some early records; deed books partially survive
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: St. Francis County formed 1827 from Phillips County. County seat is Forrest City. Located in eastern Arkansas in the delta region. The 1858 fire and 1863 Civil War losses destroyed early records.
county_metadata.research_tips: St. Francis County suffered an 1858 fire and 1863 Civil War losses that destroyed early records. The County Clerk's office in Forrest City holds surviving records. This eastern Arkansas delta county was a major cotton area with a large enslaved population. Forrest City, founded in the 1860s as a railroad town, became the county seat. The St. Francis County Museum has local materials. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA are essential for African American research. The L'Anguille River valley was heavily settled before the Civil War. East Arkansas Community College in Forrest City has regional collections. FamilySearch has microfilmed surviving county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for St. Francis County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Forrest City holds deed books from 1827

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Stone County formed 1873 from Independence, Izard, Searcy, and Van Buren counties. County seat is Mountain View, known as the 'Folk Music Capital of the World.' Home to the Ozark Folk Center State Park.
county_metadata.research_tips: Stone County records at the County Clerk's office in Mountain View are complete from 1873. Mountain View is the 'Folk Music Capital of the World' and home to the Ozark Folk Center State Park, which documents traditional Ozark culture. The county's remoteness preserved traditional folkways and family patterns. The Stone County Historical Society and the Stone County Library have local materials. The Blanchard Springs Caverns area has attracted visitors since the 1800s. Cemetery surveys of rural family burial grounds are particularly important. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Stone County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Mountain View holds deed books from 1873

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Union County formed 1829 from Clark and Hempstead counties. County seat is El Dorado. The 1921 oil boom transformed El Dorado from a small cotton town to a boomtown virtually overnight.
county_metadata.research_tips: Union County records at the County Clerk's office in El Dorado are well-preserved from 1829. El Dorado experienced a massive oil boom in 1921 that transformed the county from agricultural to industrial almost overnight. The South Arkansas Historical Preservation Society and the South Arkansas Arts Center have local history collections. Pre-oil Union County was a major cotton and timber area with significant slaveholding. Estate inventories in probate records list enslaved persons. Freedmen's Bureau records are available at NARA. The Murphy Oil Company records document oil-era families. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of Union County records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Union County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in El Dorado holds deed books from 1829

source_profiles.source_name: Union County Deed Books -- County Clerk / Arkansas History Commission
source_profiles.repository: Union County Clerk, El Dorado / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Union County recording land conveyances, mortgages, and property transfers; County Clerk's office is the primary repository; Arkansas History Commission holds microfilm copies
source_profiles.survival_notes: Deed books complete from 1829; housed at County Clerk's office in El Dorado

source_profiles.source_name: Union County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Union County; slave schedules document the significant slaveholding in southwest Arkansas

source_profiles.source_name: Union County Probate Records -- County Clerk / Arkansas History Commission
source_profiles.repository: Union County Clerk, El Dorado / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, inventories, administrations, and estate records for Union County; estate inventories frequently list enslaved persons by name, age, and value -- essential for African American genealogy

source_profiles.source_name: Union County Marriage Records -- County Clerk / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Union County Clerk, El Dorado / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records for Union County including bonds, licenses, and certificates; County Clerk's office is the repository

source_profiles.source_name: Union County Federal Land Patents -- BLM / GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents (original entries) for Union County from the General Land Office; Arkansas is a public land state and most original land titles derive from federal patents; searchable by county and patentee name at BLM website

source_profiles.source_name: Union County Freedmen's Bureau Records -- NARA / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Freedmen's Bureau records for Union County including labor contracts, marriage records, school reports, and complaints; essential for tracing African American families in the transition from slavery to freedom

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Union County churches and family burial grounds; Arkansas Gravestone Project and Find A Grave provide online access; local genealogical societies have published transcription volumes
